source-discipline.reason: The record quotes the owner directive verbatim from the same session turn; the ratified roster restates the package's grep-recounted 16 (verified twice this session, incl. once by status-header-scoped grep after a loose grep disagreed); every execution claim in the companion register is classed EXECUTED (with the session's actual edits), STAGED (with the exact command and the reason it is owner-run: two permission-layer denials, quoted in-session), or QUEUED - no ratified item is silently dropped.
source-discipline.notes: The record documents that C1 was NOT executed by the agent: the permission classifier twice declined the canonical-DB mutation and '[No preference]' on the explicit prompt was treated as insufficient authorization. The DB remains at user_version 25 until the owner runs the staged command.

bias_direction: A ratification record authored by the party whose work is being ratified risks reading breadth into the directive ('resolve all') that the owner did not intend - especially on B5 (previously recommended either-way) and B8's currency-header fill. Bounded by: the record names both interpretive calls explicitly rather than burying them; the one irreversible action was NOT taken under the broad directive when the permission layer required specific authorization; and every interpretation is one owner correction away from reversal, with the register giving each item a place to be countermanded.
independent_reviewer_counterclaim: A reviewer might argue 'resolve all accept ratify all commit all' is too terse to ratify 16 decision records with sub-items, and a per-item confirmation should have been demanded. Response: the package was explicitly designed for one-sitting ratification with per-item replies OFFERED; the owner chose the blanket form against that exact menu after seven days of incremental review in the same thread; and the two genuinely irreversible or externally-visible acts (canonical DB mutation, CI enforcement) were still handled separately - one blocked pending direct owner execution, one left as an explicit open owner action.
